blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XIX
NOETHER'S THEOREMby P. Deligne
Contents

1.Statement of the theorems1
2.Complement to XVIII4
3.Proof of 1.35
4.Second complement to XVIII8
5.The exceptional cases of Noether's theorem.11
1. Statement of the theorems
The present exposé is a modernization of Lefschetz's proof [3] page 108 of Noether's theorem (1.2) below.

1.1. Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(p\). Let \(d\geq1\) and \(\underline a=(a_i)_{1\leq i\leq d}\) be a sequence of integers \(\geq1\). A complete intersection \(V\) of dimension \(n\) and multidegree \(\underline a\) in the projective space \(\mathbb P^{n+d}\) over \(k\) will be called generic if, over an extension of \(k\), it is projectively isomorphic to a complete intersection defined by equations whose coefficients are algebraically independent over the prime field. In what follows, and unless expressly mentioned otherwise, we assume that \(d\geq1\) and that \(a_i\geq2\).

Theorem 1.2.

Let \(V\subset\mathbb P^{2+d}\) be a generic complete intersection surface of multidegree \(\underline a\). We assume that \(\underline a\ne(2),(3),(2,2)\). Then every (reduced) curve lying on \(V\) is the (scheme-theoretic) intersection of \(V\) and a hypersurface of \(\mathbb P^{2+d}\).
Let us show that the theorem follows from its corollary
\[ \tag{1.2.1}\operatorname{Pic}(\mathbb P^{2+d})=\mathbb Z\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Pic}(V). \]

If \(C\) is a curve lying on \(V\), (1.2.1) implies that \(\mathcal O(C)\simeq\mathcal O(n)\) for suitable \(n\); if \(H\) is a hyperplane section, \(n=(\prod a_i)^{-1}(C,H)>0\). It remains to prove that the linear system induced on \(V\) by \(|nH|\) is complete. Let us write \(V\) as an intersection of hypersurfaces \(H_i\) of degree \(a_i\) and let \(V_i=H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_i\). One verifies by induction on \(i\) that the linear system induced by \(|nH|\) on \(V_i\) is complete: in the exact sequence
\[ H^0(V_i,\mathcal O(n))\longrightarrow H^0(V_{i+1},\mathcal O(n)) \longrightarrow H^1(V_i,\mathcal O(n-a_{i+1})), \]
the last group is zero (FAC).

Theorem 1.3.

Let \(V\subset\mathbb P^{2n+d}\) be a generic complete intersection of even dimension \(2n\ne0\) and multidegree \(\underline a\). We assume that one of the following conditions is satisfied

  1. \(a\) \(k\) is not of characteristic 2, and \(2n;\underline a\) is distinct from (2n;2), (2n;2,2), \((2;3)\).
  2. \(b\) the Hodge number \(h^{2n,0}\) is nonzero, i.e. \(\sum(a_i-1)>2n\).

Then every algebraic cohomology class in \(H^{2n}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) is a rational multiple of the class \(\eta^n\) of the linear sections.

Let us deduce 1.2.1 from 1.3. The exceptional cases in 1.2 are exactly those for which \(\sum(a_i-1)\leq2\); we can therefore apply 1.3 \(b\) to the surface \(V\) of 1.2. We know that \(\operatorname{Pic}^\tau(V)=0\) (XI 1.8); hence that \(\operatorname{Pic}(V)\) injects into \(H^2(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(1))\). By 1.3, every element of \(\operatorname{Pic}(V)\) is a rational multiple of the class \(\eta\) of \(\mathcal O(1)\), and we conclude by (XI 1.8).
It is clear that the validity of 1.2 and 1.3 does not depend on the generic complete intersection chosen, but only on \(p,n\) and \(\underline a\).

Theorem 1.4.

Let \(X\subset\mathbb P\) be a smooth projective variety purely of dimension \(2n+1\) over \(k\). Let \(\ell\subset\check{\mathbb P}\) be a Lefschetz pencil of hyperplane sections of \(X\). For \(k\) of characteristic 2, we assume that \(\ell\) is even a generic Lefschetz pencil.

We assume that \(X\) satisfies (LV) (XVIII 5.2.2).

Let \(V\) be the generic hyperplane section in the pencil \(\ell\). One of the following two conditions is satisfied.

  1. \(a\) No cohomology class \(\ne0\) in the vanishing part \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) of the cohomology group \(H^{2n}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) is algebraic.
  2. \(b\) The algebraic cohomology classes generate \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\).
Let us make precise the meaning of the word generic in 1.4.

\(a\) Let \(G\) be the Grassmannian of lines of the projective space \(\check{\mathbb P}\) dual to \(\mathbb P\). Let us call a «geometric point» of \(G\) a morphism from the spectrum of an algebraically closed field to \(G\). Every geometric point \(\bar s\to G\) defines a pencil of hyperplane sections \(\ell\) of the variety deduced from \(X\) by extension of scalars from \(k\) to \(k(\bar s)\). We say that \(\ell\) is generic if \(\bar s\) is localized at the generic point of \(G\).

\(b\) Let \(\ell\) be a pencil of hyperplane sections (a line in \(\check{\mathbb P}\)). Every geometric point \(\bar s\to\ell\) defines a hyperplane section of the variety deduced from \(X\) by extension of scalars from \(k\) to \(k(\bar s)\). We say that it is generic if \(\bar s\) is localized at the generic point of \(\ell\).

Let \(\bar s\) be as above, with image in \(\ell\) the generic point \(s\) and \(S\subset\ell\) the finite set of points of \(\ell\) giving rise to a singular hyperplane section. The monodromy group \(\pi_1(\ell-S,\bar s)\), a quotient of \(\operatorname{Gal}(k(\bar s)/k(s))\), acts on \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\). If \(k\) is not of characteristic 2, this action is irreducible (XVIII 6.7); in no. 2 below, we will show that this result remains valid without any hypothesis on the characteristic for a generic pencil. The subspace of \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) generated by the algebraic cohomology classes is stable under the monodromy group (i.e. under Galois); it is therefore all or nothing, which proves 1.4.

Corollary 1.5.

Let \(V\subset\mathbb P^{2n+d}\) be a generic complete intersection of even dimension (2n) and multidegree \(\underline a\). We have either

  1. \(a\) Every algebraic cohomology class in \(H^{2n}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) is a rational multiple of \(\eta^n\).
  2. \(b\) \(H^{2n}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) is generated by algebraic cohomology classes.

If \(b\) is satisfied, then for every complete intersection of dimension (2n) and multidegree \(\underline a\) over an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(p\), \(H^{2n}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) is generated by the algebraic classes.

We apply 1.4 for \(X\) a generic complete intersection of dimension (2n+1) and multidegree \((a_1\cdots a_{d-1})\) and for \(\ell\) a generic pencil of sections of \(X\) by hypersurfaces of degree \(a_d\) (cf. XVII 2.4). In fact, we could also take \(X\) of multidegree \((a_1\cdots a_d)\) and \(\ell\) a generic pencil of hyperplane sections. The final remark follows from the fact that an algebraic cohomology class remains algebraic under specialization.
2. Complement to XVIII

Proposition 2.1.

Let \(X\subset\mathbb P\) be a smooth projective variety purely of even dimension (2n) over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and \(\ell\subset\check{\mathbb P}\) a generic Lefschetz pencil of hyperplane sections of \(X\). Let \(s\) be a geometric point of \(\ell\), \(H_s\) the corresponding hyperplane section, \(S\subset\ell\) the set of exceptional values of the pencil and \(\pi_1(\ell-S,s)\) the monodromy group.

We assume that \(X\) satisfies (LV).

Then the representation of \(\pi_1(\ell-S,s)\) on the vanishing cohomology \(\operatorname{Ev}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\subset H^{2n}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is absolutely irreducible.

Let us recall that \(\operatorname{Ev}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is the orthogonal complement of the image of \(H^{2n}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) in \(H^{2n}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\), an image equal to the subgroup of invariants under the monodromy group (XVIII 5.6.10).

The projective line \(\mathbb P^1\) is simply connected. The group \(\pi_1(\ell-S,s)\) is therefore (topologically) generated by the inertia subgroups at the points of \(S\) — and their conjugates. Its image in \(\operatorname{GL}(H^n(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell))\) is therefore (topologically) generated by the \(x\longmapsto x-(-1)^n(x,\delta)\delta\), for \(\delta\) conjugate to a vanishing cycle. The image of \(H^{2n}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) in \(H^{2n}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is therefore the orthogonal complement of the subspace generated by the vanishing cycles and their conjugates: \(\operatorname{Ev}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is generated by the vanishing cycles and their conjugates.
For a generic Lefschetz pencil, the arguments of no. 6 of XVIII show again that the vanishing cycles \(\delta\) relative to the various points of \(S\) are conjugate to one another.

Let \(V\subset\operatorname{Ev}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) be an invariant subspace \(\ne0\). There exist a conjugate \(\delta\) of a vanishing cycle and \(v\in V\) such that \((v,\delta)\ne0\) (it is here that we use (LV)). Since \(v-(-1)^n(v,\delta)\delta\in V\), we have \(\delta\in V\). The subspace \(V\), containing a vanishing cycle (and all its conjugates), coincides with \(\operatorname{Ev}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\).
This argument remains valid after extension of the coefficient field \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), whence the absolute irreducibility.

Remark 2.2. When the hypothesis (LV) is omitted, the preceding arguments still prove that every invariant subspace of \(H^{2n}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) contains \(\operatorname{Ev}(H_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) or is contained in the image of \(H^{2n}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\). The same result holds for \(X\) of even dimension (and any Lefschetz pencil).

3.1. We must prove that, under the hypotheses of the theorem, 1.5 \(b\) is not satisfied. The case where \(h^{0,2n}\ne0\) will be treated by Katz in XXI 4.1 (the condition \(h^{0,2n}\ne0\) is equivalent to \(\sum d_i-(2n+r+1)\geq0\), this number being the integer \(m\) such that \(\Omega_V^{2n}\simeq\mathcal O(m)\)). The results of the present exposé will not be used again in the rest of the seminar, so there is no vicious circle.

3.2. When \(k=\mathbb C\), one proves 1.3 by noting that if 1.5 \(b\) is satisfied, the cohomology of \(V\) is purely of type \((n,n)\), the algebraic cohomology being of type \((n,n)\). This is the case only in the exceptional cases excluded from 1.3 (XI 2.9). By the Lefschetz principle, 1.3 is therefore true in characteristic 0.

Proposition 3.3.

Let us place ourselves under the hypotheses of 1.4, and suppose \(b\) satisfied. Let \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\) be the \(\mathbb Q\)-vector subspace of \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) generated by the algebraic cohomology classes.

\(a\) We have \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\). On \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\), the intersection form is rational and definite, of sign \((-1)^n\).

\(b\) The vanishing cycles and their conjugates are in \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\); there they form an irreducible root system of type A, D or E.

\(c\) The monodromy group (the image of \(\pi_1(\ell-S,s)\) in \(\operatorname{GL}(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q))\)) is finite; it is the Weyl group of the root system \(b\).

Except for the definiteness of the intersection form, \(a\) follows from the fact that the intersection form is nondegenerate on \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) (by (LV)) and rational on \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\).

The condition 1.4 \(b\) is equivalent to saying that there exist on \(V\) algebraic cycles modulo numerical equivalence not invariant under the monodromy. This condition is therefore independent of \(\ell\). Let \(x\) be such an algebraic cycle, and \(\delta\) the vanishing cycle relative to \(s_0\in S\) and to a path \(ch\) from \(\bar s\) (defining \(V\)) to \(s_0\). For suitable \(ch\), \((x,\delta)\ne0\). The Galois transform \(\sigma(x)=x-(-1)^n(x,\delta)\delta\) of \(x\) is still algebraic; \((x,\delta)\delta\) is therefore algebraic, the image of a cycle \(y\) independent of \(\ell\). The rational number \(\frac{(-1)^n}{2}(y,y)=(x,\delta)^2\) is therefore independent of \(\ell\); it is a square in all the fields \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), hence a square, and \(\pm(x,\delta)\) is in \(\mathbb Q\) and independent of \(\ell\). This shows not only that \(\delta\in\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\), but also that the algebraic cycle modulo numerical equivalence \(\pm\delta=(\sigma(x)-x)/(x,\delta)\) is independent of \(\ell\).

An algebraic cycle on a variety \(V\) is always defined over a finite extension of any field of definition of \(V\). It follows that \(\pi_1(\ell-S,s)\) acts on \(\operatorname{Ev}(V,\mathbb Q)\) via a finite group. This group is generated by the reflections associated with the vanishing cycles \(\delta\) and their conjugates (XVIII 6.6.1 and no. 2). The \(\delta\) and their conjugates therefore form a root system whose Weyl group is the monodromy group. Moreover, all the roots of this root system are conjugate under the Weyl group: it is of type A, D or E.
The intersection form is, up to a factor, the unique form invariant under the Weyl group. It is therefore definite. Its sign is determined by computing \[(\delta,\delta)=(-1)^n2.\]

Proposition 3.4.

If \(k=\mathbb C\), and under the hypotheses of 1.4, the following conditions are equivalent

(i) the monodromy group is finite;

(ii) the vanishing cohomology \(\operatorname{Ev}(V)\) is purely of type \((n,n)\).

Let \(\widetilde X\to\ell\) be the bundle whose fibers are the hyperplane sections belonging to the pencil \(\ell\), and \(V_s\) the fiber at \(s\in\ell(\mathbb C)\). The \(\operatorname{Ev}(V_s)\) form an algebraic variation of Hodge structures on \(\ell\). By the theorem of the fixed part [2] 4.1.2 applied to a finite covering of \(\ell\) (cf. [2] 4.1.3.3), (i) implies that the Hodge structure of the \(\operatorname{Ev}(V_s)\) is locally constant. The monodromy group therefore acts by automorphisms of the Hodge structure. For \(T\) a Picard-Lefschetz transformation, \(\operatorname{Im}(T-1)\) is a Hodge substructure of rank one, hence of type \((n,n)\), generated by a vanishing cycle \(\delta\). The vanishing cycles being of type \((n,n)\), all of \(\operatorname{Ev}(V_s)\) is of that type.
Conversely, if \(\operatorname{Ev}(V)\) is purely of type \((n,n)\), the intersection form on \(\operatorname{Ev}(V)\) is definite and the monodromy group finite (cf. [2] 4.1.3.3).

Remark 3.5. If the equivalent conditions of 3.3 are satisfied, one sees as in 3.3 that the vanishing cycles (and their conjugates) form a root system of type A, D or E whose Weyl group is the monodromy group.

3.6. Let us prove 1.3 for \(k\) of finite characteristic \(\ne2\). Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring of unequal characteristic with residue field \(k\), and \(X=\mathbb P_S^{2n+d}\) a complete intersection of relative dimension \(2n+1\) and multidegree \((a_1\ldots a_{d-1})\) over \(S\). Let \(\ell\) be a Lefschetz pencil of sections of \(X\) by hypersurfaces of degree \(a_d\) (in the special fiber, \(\ell_s\) is a Lefschetz pencil for \(X_s\)). Let \(f:\widetilde X\to\ell\simeq\mathbb P_S^1\) be the bundle whose fibers are the sections of the pencil. The exceptional locus \(T\subset\ell\) is étale over \(S\) (XVII 6; we use here that \(p\ne2\)). The sheaf \(R^{2n}f_*\mathbb Q_\ell\) is locally constant on \(\ell-T\); it is therefore tamely ramified and its monodromy is the same in characteristic \(p\) and in characteristic 0. If 1.5 \(b\) is satisfied, we therefore deduce from 3.3 \(c\) and 3.4 that the cohomology group \(H^{2n}(V)\) of the complex complete intersections of dimension \(2n\) and multidegree \(\underline a\) is purely of type \((n,n)\), which happens only in the cases excluded by 1.3 (XI 2.9).
4. Second complement to XVIII

In this number, we work over \(\mathbb C\) and in integral (transcendental) cohomology. We take up again, with methods closer to those of Lefschetz, some of the results of XVIII. In XVIII, they were proved only after tensoring with \(\mathbb Q\) (or rather \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), which amounts to the same thing).

4.1. Let \(X\subset\mathbb P\) be a smooth complex projective variety purely of dimension \(n+1\) and \(\ell\) a Lefschetz pencil of hyperplane sections of \(X\), with axis \(A\). We also denote by \(\ell\) the parameter space of the pencil, isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\). Let \(S\subset\ell\) be the set of exceptional values. For \(s\in\ell\), we will denote by \(H_s\) the corresponding hyperplane section of \(X\) (of dimension \(n\)).
Let \(T(A)\) be a closed tubular neighborhood of \(A\cap X\) in \(X\), whose boundary is transverse to all the \(H_s\).

Let \(0,\infty\) be two points in \(\ell-S\), and let \(\alpha\) be an oriented diffeomorphism from \(\ell-S\) to \(\mathbb P^1(\mathbb C)\) sending \(0\) to \(0\), \(\infty\) to \(\infty\) and \(S\) into the circle of radius one centered at \(0\). The composite \(\alpha f\) of \(\alpha\) and the projection onto \(\ell\) makes \(X-T(A)^\circ\) a singular fibration in manifolds with boundary over \(\mathbb P^1(\mathbb C)\) (one singularity above each point of \(\alpha(S)\)). We will denote by \(c_s\) the line segment joining \(0\) to \(\alpha(s)\).
Omitting the mention of \(\alpha\), we have the figure
\[ \begin{array}{c} \quad s_2\qquad\quad s_1\\[-2mm] \quad\,|\qquad\diagup\\[-1mm] \quad c_2\quad c_1\\[-2mm] \qquad\backslash\!|\!\diagup\\[-1mm] \qquad 0 \end{array} \]

In the neighborhood of \(s\in S\) and of the singular point \(x_s\) of \(H_s\subset X\), we find ourselves (in suitable local coordinates) in the situation studied in XIV 3.2. If \(\alpha\) is chosen holomorphic near \(s\), we can even find local coordinates on \(X\) and \(\ell\) such that \(f\) is written \(\sum z_i^2\) and that \(\alpha^{-1}(c_s)\) is « \(z\) real positive ». Above the intersection of a neighborhood of \(s\) and of \(\alpha^{-1}(c_s)-s\), we can then consider the bundle of real spheres \(\Delta'_s\subset X\), defined by « \(z_i\) real ». For \(t\to s\), the fiber \(\Delta'_{st}\) of \(\Delta'_s\) at \(t\) tends to \(x_s\). We will denote by \(\Delta_s\) the union of \(\{x_s\}\) and of a sphere bundle above \(\alpha^{-1}(c_s)-\{s\}\), contained in \(X-T(A)\) and extending \(\Delta'_s\). \(\Delta_s\) is a disk embedded in \(X\), whose boundary \(H_0\cap\Delta_s\) is a sphere representing the vanishing cycle \(\delta_s\) at \(s\), transported into \(H_0\) along the path \(\alpha^{-1}(c_s)\).
Let us apply Morse theory to the function \(|\alpha f|^2\), in order to study how the homotopy of \[\{x\in X\mid x\in T(A)\ \text{or}\ |\alpha f(x)|^2\le r\}\]
changes as \(r\) grows. Let \[X^- = \{x\in X\mid x\in T(A)\ \text{or}\ \alpha f(x)\ne\infty\}.\]

We find that the inclusion \[H_0\cup\bigcup_{s\in S}\Delta_s\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T(A)\cup H_0\cup\bigcup_{s\in S}\Delta_s\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X^-\] is a homotopy equivalence. We know, again by Morse theory, that \(X-X^-\) can be retracted by deformation onto a subcomplex of real dimension \(\le n\). In total, we therefore have the following result

Theorem 4.2.

There exists a subcomplex \(\Sigma\), compact and of (real) dimension \(\le n\), of \(H_\infty^{\mathrm{aff}}=H_\infty-A\) such that the inclusion

\[H_0\cup\bigcup_{s\in S}\Delta_s\longrightarrow X-\Sigma\]

is a homotopy equivalence.

4.3. The subtraction of \(\Sigma\) has no effect on the cohomology, homology or homotopy groups of index \(i\le n\) of \(X\). Since \(H_0\cup\bigcup_{s\in S}\Delta_s\) is deduced from \(H_0\) by attaching \(n+1\)-disks along spheres representing the vanishing cycles, we find in homology \[\mathbb Z^S\longrightarrow H_n(H_0)\longrightarrow H_n(X)\longrightarrow0.\]
The analogous exact sequence in cohomology, and the one deduced from it by Poincaré duality, form a commutative diagram

\[ \tag{4.3.1} \begin{array}{ccccccccc} &&\mathbb Z^S&&&&\\ &&\uparrow\scriptstyle{(\ ,\delta_s)}&&&&\\ \mathbb Z^S&\xrightarrow{\ \sum n_s\delta_s\ }&H^n(H_0)&\longrightarrow&H^{n+2}(X)&\longrightarrow&0\\ &&\uparrow&&\nearrow\scriptstyle{\eta\wedge}&&\\ &&H^n(X)&&&&\\ &&\uparrow&&&&\\ &&0&&&& \end{array} \]

pages.title: 5. The exceptional cases of Noether's theorem

blocks.html: 5. The exceptional cases of Noether's theorem
In this number, we continue to work over \(\mathbb C\), in integral cohomology.

5.1. Let \(2n\), \(d\) and \(\underline a=(a_1,\ldots,a_d)\) (\(d\geq1\), \(a_i\geq2\)) be such that the cohomology in dimension \(2n\) of the smooth complete intersections \(V\) of dimension \(2n\) and multi- degree \(\underline a\) is purely of type \((n,n)\). In other words, \(n=0\) or \((2n;\underline a)\) is equal to \((2n;2)\), \((2n;2,2)\) or \((2;3)\) (XI 2.9).

Suppose that \(V\) is deduced from a complete intersection \(X\subset\mathbb P^{2n+d}\) (resp. \(X\subset\mathbb P^{2n+d+1}\)) of dimension \(2n+1\) and multidegree \((a_1,\ldots,a_{d-1})\) (resp. \((a_1,\ldots,a_d)\)) by taking a section by a hypersurface of degree \(a_d\) (resp. a hyperplane section) belonging to a Lefschetz pencil \(\ell\).

The vanishing part \(\operatorname{Ev}(V)\) of \(H^{2n}(V)\), equal to the primitive part of this cohomology, is, by 4.3.1, generated over \(\mathbb Z\) by the vanishing cycles. It is the orthogonal complement of the \(\eta^n\) power of the cohomology class \(\eta\) of a hyperplane section.

Since on \(H^{2n}(V)\) the intersection form is unimodular, and since \(\eta^{2n}\) is not the multiple \(b y\) (\(b>1\)) of another cohomology class (XI 1.6 (iv)), the discriminant of the intersection form on \(\operatorname{Ev}(V)\) is equal to \[(\eta^n,\eta^n)=\prod a_i.\]
Let \(R\) be the root system in \(H^{2n}(V)\) formed by the vanishing cycles and their conjugates. We have the following information about \(R\)
\(a\) \(R\) is empty or of type A, D or E;
\(b\) the rank of \(R\) is given in XI 1.10;

\(c\) the discriminant is \(\prod a_i\). In the tables, this discriminant is the order of the center of the corresponding simply connected complex simple group. The argument by which we computed the discriminant shows that this center, dual to the quotient of the weight lattice by the root lattice, is cyclic.
In all the cases at hand, this information determines \(R\) completely if \(n\ne0\). We find:

Proposition 5.2.

(i) For \(V\) a quadric of even dimension, \(R\) is of type \(A_1\).

(ii) For \(V\) a cubic surface, \(R\) is of type \(E_6\).

(iii) For \(V\) of dimension \(2n\), and the intersection of two quadrics, \(R\) is of type \(D_{2n+3}\).

5.3. Suppose that \(n\ne0\). The set \(R^1\) of vectors of length \(\pm2\) in \(\operatorname{Ev}(V)\) is also a root system, having the same characteristics \(a\) \(b\) \(c\) as \(R\). We therefore have \(R=R^1\). Let \(\Pi_1\) be the fundamental group of the parameter space of all the complete intersections \(V\subset\mathbb P^{2n+d}\) of the type considered. The image of \(\Pi_1\) in \(\operatorname{GL}(\operatorname{Ev}(V))\) is the Weyl group of \(R\). In all the cases at hand, it is the automorphism group of the triple \[(H^{2n}(V),\eta^n,\text{ intersection form}).\]

5.4. With \(R\) replaced by \(R^1\) of 5.3, the result 4.2 (ii) is well known [1] and, for small values of \(n\), 4.2 (iii) was known, at least to Manin (\(n=0\) is trivial; a surface that is an intersection of two quadrics is a del Pezzo surface). A detailed study of the intersection of two quadrics was made by M. Reid [4]. He deduces 4.2 (iii) from a study of the geometry of the linear subspaces of dimension \(n\) on an intersection of two quadrics \(V\subset\mathbb P^{2n+2}\).
Bibliography

[1] E. CARTAN. Quelques remarques sur les 28 bitangentes d’une quartique plane et les 27 droites d’une surface cubique.
Bull. Sc. Math. 70 1946 p. 42-45, Oeuvres, partie I, tome 2 p. 1353.
[2] P. DELIGNE. Théorie de Hodge II. Publ. Math. IHES 40, 1971 p. 5-58.

[3] S. LEFSCHETZ. L’analysis situs et la géométrie algébrique.
Gauthier-Villars 1924 (reissued in Selected papers - Chelsea).
[4] M. REID. Thesis, Cambridge 1972.
[5] J. P. SERRE. Faisceaux algébriques cohérents. Ann. of Math. LXI (1955) p. 197-278 (cited as FAC)
